content.text: An autoregressive model of order p, written AR(p), expresses the current value as a linear combination of the previous p values plus white noise:

c is a constant (the unconditional mean times 1 − Σφᵢ), φ₁, …, φₚ are the autoregressive coefficients, and εₜ is the innovation — the unpredictable part. If all roots of the characteristic polynomial 1 − φ₁z − … − φₚzᵖ lie outside the unit circle in the complex plane, the process is stationary: it has constant mean and variance regardless of when you observe it.

import numpy as np

def simulate_ar(phi, c, sigma, T, rng=None):
    """
    phi:  array of AR coefficients (length p)
    c:    constant term
    sigma: noise std dev
    T:    number of time steps to generate
    """
    rng = rng or np.random.default_rng()
    p = len(phi)
    x = np.zeros(T)
    # Initialise with zeros (burn-in discarded in practice)
    for t in range(p, T):
        x[t] = c + phi @ x[t-p:t][::-1] + rng.normal(0, sigma)
    return x[p:]

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
# AR(2): x_t = 0.6 x_{t-1} + 0.2 x_{t-2} + ε
x = simulate_ar(phi=np.array([0.6, 0.2]), c=0.0, sigma=1.0, T=1000, rng=rng)
print(f"mean={x.mean():.3f}  std={x.std():.3f}")
# mean≈0, std≈1.3 (higher than noise because autocorrelation amplifies)
A moving average model of order q expresses the current value as a linear combination of the current and previous q innovations:

MA models have autocorrelation that cuts off exactly at lag q — a sharp diagnostic signature. They are always stationary (any finite MA process is stationary), but not all MA processes are invertible.
ARMA(p, q) combines autoregressive and moving average components:
Often a low-order ARMA(1,1) or ARMA(2,1) can represent a pattern that would require a much higher-order pure AR or MA model, leading to more parsimonious fits.

Many real-world series (prices, cumulative counts) have a trending mean — they are non-stationary. ARIMA(p, d, q) extends ARMA by differencing the series d times to remove trends. The differenced series Δᵈxₜ is then modelled as ARMA(p, q).
PACF cuts off at lag p
ACF cuts off at lag q
Both ACF and PACF tail off gradually
Non-stationary with linear trend (d=1) or quadratic (d=2)

# pip install statsmodels
import numpy as np
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
# Simulate AR(2) series
phi_true = np.array([0.7, -0.2])
T = 300
x = np.zeros(T)
for t in range(2, T):
    x[t] = phi_true @ x[t-2:t][::-1] + rng.normal(0, 1)

# Fit ARIMA(2,0,0) = AR(2)
result = ARIMA(x, order=(2, 0, 0)).fit()
print(result.summary().tables[1])  # coefficient table
print(f"AR1={result.arparams[0]:.3f}  AR2={result.arparams[1]:.3f}")
# AR1≈0.70  AR2≈-0.20

# Forecast next 5 steps
forecast = result.forecast(steps=5)
print("Forecast:", forecast.round(2))
Choosing p and q is a model selection problem. Two standard criteria penalise log-likelihood by model complexity:

where k is the number of free parameters and T is the sample size. BIC penalises complexity more heavily and tends to select sparser models. In practice, fit a grid of (p, q) values and pick the order with the lowest AIC or BIC:

import warnings
import itertools

best_aic = np.inf
best_order = None
for p, q in itertools.product(range(5), range(4)):
    try:
        with warnings.catch_warnings():
            warnings.simplefilter("ignore")
            res = ARIMA(x, order=(p, 0, q)).fit()
        if res.aic < best_aic:
            best_aic   = res.aic
            best_order = (p, 0, q)
    except Exception:
        pass
print(f"Best order by AIC: ARIMA{best_order}  AIC={best_aic:.2f}")
# Best order by AIC: ARIMA(2, 0, 0)  AIC=...

ARMA models are probabilistic generative models: once the parameters (φ, θ, σ) are estimated, you can sample new sequences by iterating the defining equation forward in time with fresh innovations drawn from N(0, σ²). This is the same autoregressive generation principle used by neural language models — predict the next token (or value), sample from the predicted distribution, append to context, repeat.

The main difference is that ARMA parametrises the conditional distribution as a linear function of past values, while neural models use deep non-linear functions. Linear models are interpretable and fit in milliseconds on small datasets; neural models scale to high-dimensional, complex time series at the cost of compute and interpretability.
Why Autoregressive Time Series Models Matter

Autoregressive models are the canonical statistical tool for time series because they formalise a fundamental property of most real sequences: the present depends on the recent past. Stock prices exhibit momentum and mean-reversion; temperature follows seasonal cycles with autoregressive persistence; network traffic has daily and weekly periodicity layered on top of second-to-second autocorrelation. Autoregressive models capture these patterns as explicit, interpretable parameters rather than as opaque neural weights.

For generative AI, AR(p) models are the conceptual ancestor of modern autoregressive neural language models. GPT generates tokens exactly as AR(p) generates values: the current output is a function of previous outputs, with the distributional family (linear Gaussian in ARMA, deep non-linear in GPT) being the key difference. Understanding ARMA models provides the statistical foundation for understanding why autoregressive neural generation works, what its failure modes are, and when simpler statistical alternatives are preferable.
ARMA models have closed-form likelihoods (Gaussian) that can be computed via the Kalman filter in O(T) time, enabling exact maximum likelihood estimation.
Each coefficient φᵢ directly quantifies the linear influence of the i-step-ago value on the current value — no feature engineering or attention pattern analysis required.
ACF and PACF plots provide clear signatures that identify the correct (p, q) order before model fitting begins.
Prediction intervals from ARIMA are exact Gaussian intervals when the model is correctly specified — no simulation or bootstrapping required.
An ARIMA(2,1,1) has 4 free parameters and can be reliably estimated from as few as 50 observations. Neural models require thousands.
Generating H-step-ahead forecasts from ARIMA is O(H·p) — microseconds for typical p and H.
ARMA assumes the conditional mean is a linear function of past values. Non-linear dynamics (threshold effects, volatility clustering) require GARCH, threshold AR, or neural models.
The model assumes iid Gaussian innovations. Financial returns exhibit fat tails and volatility clustering that violate this assumption.
AR and MA models require a stationary series. Identifying the correct differencing order d requires stationarity tests (ADF, KPSS) and domain knowledge.
Standard ARIMA models one series at a time. Multivariate dynamics (e.g., how sales and marketing spend interact) require VAR or VARIMA.

ARIMA forecasts degrade rapidly beyond a few periods ahead because uncertainty compounds multiplicatively. For long-horizon forecasting, neural models or Bayesian structural models often outperform ARIMA.
When to Use Autoregressive Time Series Models

Use ARIMA when: the series is univariate with weak to moderate non-stationarity (handled by differencing); you have fewer than 1000 observations; you need interpretable parameters or exact confidence intervals; the series appears to have stable autocorrelation structure (ACF and PACF are well-behaved); or you are forecasting 1-12 steps ahead.

Do NOT use ARIMA when: the series has strong seasonality (use SARIMA or Prophet instead); you observe heteroscedasticity (volatility clustering, use GARCH); you have multiple interacting series (use VAR); you need very long horizon forecasts; or you have thousands of series to model simultaneously (use neural forecasting models like N-BEATS or Temporal Fusion Transformer).

attrs.alt: ACF and PACF diagnostic plots for AR(2): ACF tails off gradually, PACF cuts off at lag 2 — the standard signature for order selection

content.text: Diagnostic Analysis: Reading ACF and PACF
Before fitting any model, plot the ACF and PACF of the (possibly differenced) series. The pattern identifies the appropriate model class:
Seasonal spikes at multiples of s

import numpy as np
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller, kpss
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
# AR(2): phi1=0.6, phi2=0.2
T = 500
x = np.zeros(T)
for t in range(2, T):
    x[t] = 0.6 * x[t-1] + 0.2 * x[t-2] + rng.normal()

# ── Stationarity test ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
adf = adfuller(x)
print(f"ADF statistic: {adf[0]:.3f}  p-value: {adf[1]:.4f}")
# ADF statistic: -14.8  p-value: 0.0000  → stationary, no differencing needed

# ── ACF/PACF plots (shows order selection) ────────────────────────────────────
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))
plot_acf(x,  lags=15, ax=axes[0], title="ACF — tails off (AR signature)")
plot_pacf(x, lags=15, ax=axes[1], title="PACF — cuts off at lag 2 (→ AR(2))")
plt.tight_layout()
# plt.savefig("acf_pacf.png")
Real-World Example: Forecasting Monthly Airline Passengers

The classic airline passenger dataset (Box and Jenkins, 1976) has a strong seasonal trend and multiplicative heteroscedasticity. It requires a log transformation to stabilise variance and SARIMA to handle the seasonal component.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller
import warnings

# ── Load dataset ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# pip install statsmodels
from statsmodels.datasets import get_rdataset
data = get_rdataset("AirPassengers").data
passengers = pd.Series(
    data["AirPassengers"].values,
    index=pd.date_range("1949-01", periods=144, freq="MS"),
)

# ── Log-transform to stabilise multiplicative seasonality ─────────────────────
log_passengers = np.log(passengers)

# ── Fit SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)[12] ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# d=1: remove trend;  D=1: remove seasonal trend;  s=12: monthly seasonality
with warnings.catch_warnings():
    warnings.simplefilter("ignore")
    model = SARIMAX(
        log_passengers,
        order=(1, 1, 1),
        seasonal_order=(1, 1, 1, 12),
        enforce_stationarity=False,
        enforce_invertibility=False,
    )
    result = model.fit(disp=False)

print(result.summary().tables[0])
print(f"AIC: {result.aic:.2f}")

# ── Forecast 24 months ahead ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
forecast_log = result.forecast(steps=24)
forecast_raw = np.exp(forecast_log)  # undo log transform

print("\nForecast (passengers, next 24 months):")
print(forecast_raw.round(0).astype(int).values)

# ── Residual diagnostics ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
residuals = result.resid
print(f"\nResidual mean:  {residuals.mean():.4f} (should be ~0)")
print(f"Residual std:   {residuals.std():.4f}")

# Ljung-Box test: p-value > 0.05 → residuals are white noise (good)
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox
lb = acorr_ljungbox(residuals, lags=[10, 20], return_df=True)
print("\nLjung-Box test:")
print(lb)
Fitting ARIMA to a non-stationary series without differencing

import numpy as np
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA

# Series with a trend (non-stationary)
x = np.cumsum(np.random.randn(200)) + np.arange(200) * 0.5

# WRONG: fit AR directly to non-stationary series
bad_model = ARIMA(x, order=(2, 0, 0)).fit()
print(bad_model.arparams)   # Coefficients will be near 1.0 (unit-root behaviour)

# RIGHT: difference first (d=1) to remove trend, then fit AR
good_model = ARIMA(x, order=(2, 1, 0)).fit()
print(good_model.arparams)  # Now identifies the genuine autocorrelation structure
Using AIC to compare models with different differencing orders

# WRONG: comparing AIC of ARIMA(2,0,0) vs ARIMA(2,1,0)
# The log-likelihoods are computed on different time series (x vs Δx)
# so AIC values are not comparable across different d values.
aic_d0 = ARIMA(x, order=(2, 0, 0)).fit().aic
aic_d1 = ARIMA(x, order=(2, 1, 0)).fit().aic
# DO NOT: if aic_d0 < aic_d1: use d=0

# RIGHT: choose d by stationarity test (ADF, KPSS), not AIC
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller
pval = adfuller(x)[1]
d = 0 if pval < 0.05 else 1  # difference if non-stationary
print(f"ADF p={pval:.4f}  →  d={d}")

from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox

result = ARIMA(x, order=(1, 1, 0)).fit()
lb = acorr_ljungbox(result.resid, lags=10, return_df=True)

# If any p-value < 0.05: residuals still have autocorrelation; model is misspecified
print(lb[lb["lb_pvalue"] < 0.05])  # Should be empty for a well-specified model
# If not empty: increase p or q, or add seasonal component
Always test for stationarity before model selection.
Run ADF and KPSS; they have opposite null hypotheses, so agreement between them gives higher confidence.
Use auto_arima for initial order selection.
pmdarima's auto_arima fits a grid of ARIMA(p,d,q) models and returns the one with the lowest AIC, saving manual ACF/PACF analysis time.
A well-fitted ARIMA model has white-noise residuals. Significant autocorrelation in residuals means the model is misspecified.
Evaluate forecasts on a held-out test window.
Split the last 10-20% of observations as a test set; compute MAE, RMSE, and MAPE on true out-of-sample predictions.
Series with variance proportional to level (sales, passenger counts) should be log-transformed; this converts multiplicative structure to additive structure that ARIMA handles correctly.
Monthly or quarterly data almost always requires seasonal differencing (D=1) and seasonal AR/MA terms (P, Q).

statsmodels fits ARIMA via maximum likelihood using the Kalman filter, which runs in O(T) per likelihood evaluation. Optimisation uses L-BFGS-B and typically converges in 50-200 function evaluations, making single-series fitting very fast (< 1 second for T ≤ 10 000). The computational bottleneck is fitting many series in parallel — e.g., forecasting demand for 10 000 SKUs.

attrs.latex: \text{Single ARIMA fit: } O(T \cdot k) \text{ where } k = \text{number of optimizer iterations}

content.text: For large-scale time series forecasting (thousands of series), use vectorised implementations. The pmdarima library supports multiprocessing; the statsforecast library (Nixtla) implements ARIMA in C++ and can fit 10 000 series in seconds. For millions of series, neural models like N-BEATS or the Amazon DeepAR model are preferable.

# pmdarima: auto-order selection
# pip install pmdarima
import pmdarima as pm

model = pm.auto_arima(
    x,
    seasonal=True,
    m=12,           # monthly seasonality
    stepwise=True,  # faster than exhaustive search
    information_criterion="aic",
    trace=True,     # prints each model tried
)
print(f"Best order: {model.order}  seasonal: {model.seasonal_order}")
preds, conf_int = model.predict(n_periods=12, return_conf_int=True)

# statsforecast: production-scale fitting
# pip install statsforecast
from statsforecast import StatsForecast
from statsforecast.models import AutoARIMA
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "unique_id": "series_1",
    "ds": pd.date_range("2020-01", periods=len(x), freq="MS"),
    "y": x,
})
sf = StatsForecast(models=[AutoARIMA(season_length=12)], freq="MS", n_jobs=-1)
sf.fit(df)
forecast = sf.predict(h=24)
print(forecast.head())
Many series, long horizon, no feature engineering

Autoregressive time series models in production face two principal reliability risks. First, structural breaks: a model trained on pre-pandemic data will systematically misforecast during or after a pandemic because the autocorrelation structure has changed. Monitor for structural breaks using CUSUM tests on rolling residuals; retrain when detected. Second, order selection instability: for short series (T < 100), AIC-selected orders can vary substantially across bootstrap samples of the data, making the forecast uncertain in ways that prediction intervals do not capture.

Security considerations are primarily relevant when ARIMA models are used in automated decision systems (algorithmic trading, automated replenishment). Adversarial inputs — deliberately injected anomalous values — can shift estimated parameters and corrupt forecasts. Winsorising extreme observations before fitting and monitoring parameter drift over time mitigates this.
Track residual mean and standard deviation in production; a shift signals model mis-specification or distribution shift.
Detect and flag outliers (more than 3σ from the rolling mean) before model fitting; outliers bias parameter estimates.
Empirically verify that 95% of held-out values fall within 95% prediction intervals; systematic undercoverage indicates model misspecification.
AR(p) expresses the current value as a linear combination of the past p values plus Gaussian noise; the process is stationary when all characteristic roots lie outside the unit circle.
MA(q) expresses the current value as a linear combination of the current and past q innovations; its autocorrelation function cuts off exactly at lag q.
ARMA(p,q) combines both; ARIMA(p,d,q) adds d rounds of differencing to handle non-stationarity.
ACF and PACF plots are the primary diagnostic tools: AR(p) shows a PACF that cuts off at lag p; MA(q) shows an ACF that cuts off at lag q; ARMA shows both tailing off.
Always test for stationarity (ADF, KPSS) before model selection; fit ARIMA with the correct d rather than comparing AIC across different d values.
Validate the fit by checking that residuals are white noise (Ljung-Box p > 0.05); significant residual autocorrelation means the model is under-fitted.
For seasonal series, use SARIMA; for multiplicative series, log-transform before fitting; for large-scale production, use statsforecast's C++ implementation.

ARIMA is the conceptual foundation for autoregressive neural language models: both predict the next value from the recent past; the key difference is the distributional family (linear Gaussian vs. deep non-linear).
